writing_language: Arabic (classical Arabic for philosophical and legal texts)

structural_features.text_organization: Commentary structure: short commentaries (epitome), middle commentaries (paraphrase), and long commentaries (literal). Original works follow treatise format with logical section divisions.
structural_features.argumentation_patterns: Demonstrative syllogistic structure (burhān) as the gold standard. Fivefold classification of arguments: demonstrative, dialectical, rhetorical, poetical, sophistical. Distinguishes by matter (premises), not form.
structural_features.paragraph_development: Definition → proposition → proof → conclusion. Frequent use of nested quotation from Aristotle followed by explanation and critique.

characteristic_terms: burhān (demonstration) — the highest form of logical argument
qiyās (syllogism) — but distinguished from Islamic jurisprudence qiyās
vocabulary_analysis.technical_vocabulary_ratio: High — approximately 60-70% technical philosophical and legal terminology
vocabulary_analysis.loanwords: Greek philosophical terms via Syriac translations; extensive use of Greek philosopher names (Aristotle=al-Faylasūf, Plato=Aflātūn)

sentence_patterns.length_distribution: Medium to long sentences; complex subordinate clauses; average 25-40 words per sentence in philosophical treatises
sentence_patterns.complexity_features: Frequent use of conditional clauses (in law and philosophy); extensive use of connective reasoning phrases (wa-inna, fa-inna, ammā)
sentence_patterns.voice_and_mood: Predominantly indicative; occasional optative for theological conclusions; subjunctive for hypothetical reasoning

stylistic_markers.rhetorical_devices: Minimal ornamentation; direct logical progression; occasional anaphora in key definitional passages; rhetorical questions in dialectical contexts
stylistic_markers.citation_patterns: Constant citation of Aristotle ('the Philosopher') and previous commentators (Alexander of Aphrodisias, Themistius, Ibn Sīnā)
stylistic_markers.textual_formulaic_expressions: In commentary openings: 'Regarding the text of [Aristotle]...' / 'As for [topic], the Philosopher says...'

stylistic_evolution.early_works: More legalistic in tone; compressed argument style; emphasis on jurisprudence terminology
stylistic_evolution.middle_period: Development of three-tiered commentary method; balance between exposition and critique
stylistic_evolution.late_period: Longer, more complex commentaries; greater critical engagement with predecessors; more personal remarks

language_purpose.secondary_functions: Theological polemic, medical exposition, educational instruction
language_purpose.register_distribution: High philosophical Arabic (falsafah), Islamic legal Arabic (fiqh), technical medical Arabic
